title: Factor structure and scoring of the SKT test battery
abstract:
FACTOR STRUCTURE AND SCORING OF THE SKT TEST BATTERY 
YONG S. KIM AND DONALD W. NIBBELINK 
Du Pont Merck Pharmaceutical Co. 
Wilmington, Delaware 
JOHN E. OVERALL 
University of Texas Medical School 
Houston. Texas 
The SKT (Syndrom Kurztest) has been used in the assessment of treatment 
responses in numerous clinical trials for treatment of dementia in German- 
speaking Europe. Data from 265 patients with mild to moderate Alzheimer’s 
disease in a study conducted in the U.S. were analyzed to evaluate factor 
structure, common and specific subtest content, reliability, and concurrent 
validity. Results confirm the presence of two primary factors of memory 
and attention. Test-retest reliability of the factor scores was estimated to 
be .75 and .93. Test-retest reliability of the composite SKT total score was 
.90. The correlations between the SKT memory and attention factor scores 
and the MMSE and ADAS measurements of dementia also support validity 
with regard to the broader construct of cognitive dysfunction. 
The SKT (Syndrom Kurztest) is a dementia assessment battery that originally was 
developed and standardized in German-speaking Europe. The SKT consists of nine 
subtests, each limited to 60 seconds’ duration with the aim of assessing deficits in memory 
and attention (Erzigkeit, 1989a). Many of the tasks are similar to those in more extended 
neuropsychological test batteries used in the United States, but they are scaled to brevity, 
performance modes, and difficulty levels appropriate for the dementia population. Five 
parallel forms are available to enhance utility for repeated administration. The purpose 
of this article is to provide additional review on factor structure and reliable scoring 
of the SKT, based upon the data obtained from a multicenter study conducted in the 
United States. 
The SKT has been administered to several thousand elderly dementia and/or 
psychiatric patients in Europe. Norms based on German data stratified into four age 
intervals and three levels of estimated premorbid intelligence were provided in a doctoral 
dissertation by Arnold (1983) and are included in the test manual (Erzigkeit, 1989b). 
Coefficient alpha reliabilites and factor structures obtained from analyses of several 
German data sets also are reported in that document. Based on sample sizes that ranged 
from 260 to 1,203, Arnold reported coefficient alpha reliability estimates for the com- 
posite SKT total score that ranged from .8 and above. Several factor analyses of data 
from German-speaking countries consistently have revealed two primary factors inter- 
preted as representing independent dimensions of memory and attention. 
Although the German psychometric documentation is extensive, two problems exist 
for use of the SKT in dementia research in the United States. First, essentially all publica- 
tions have been in the German language, and many are in sources not readily available 
to American investigators. Second, and perhaps more important, a question remains 
with respect to adaptation of the instrument for an English-speaking population. Erzigkeit 
(1989b) has produced an English version of the SKT Manual that has had limited 
Requests for reprints should be addressed to Donald W. Nibbelink, M.D., Ph.D., R&D Division, The 
Du Pont Merck Pharmaceutical Company, Barley Mill Plaza, Wilmington, DE 19880-0027. 
61 
 62 
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January 1993, Vol. 49, No. 1 
circulation in mimeograph form. Overall and Schaltenbrand (1992) described the in- 
strument in an English-language publication, but analyses carried out in this publica- 
tion were based on the data from three multicenter drug trials conducted in Germany. 
What has remained until this report is the evaluation of psychometric properties for 
SKT data obtained from patients in the Untied States with English-language test ad- 
ministration. 
The nine SKT subtests include naming of 12 pictured objects and 12 numerals, 
immediate and delayed recall of pictured objects, recognition of previously viewed 
pictures, rapid counting of target stimuli (symbols) embedded in a background of 
distracter stimuli, arranging and replacement of stimulus blocks according to numerals 
painted thereon, and a reversal naming task in which two letters of the alphabet are 
each to be responded to by naming the other. More complete descriptions of the subtests 
are provided by Overall and Schaltenbrand (1992). Taken together, they seem to measure 
aspects of language fluency (naming), praxis, recall and recognition memory, and 
attention/concentration on tasks with different cognitive processing requirements. Opera- 
tionally, three of the subtest scores represent counts of omission errors on memory tasks, 
and six are speed of performance measurements made by stopwatch. The correlational 
structure of performance on these diverse tasks by American patients afflicted with senile 
dementia of the Alzheimer type (SDAT) is examined in this article by means of a factor 
analysis model. 
A unique, and to some psychometricians questionable, feature of the SKT is its 
timed administration and scoring. Individuals are instructed and prompted to perform 
as rapidly as they can. A maximum of 60 seconds is allowed for each subtest. The score 
recorded for all subtests except the three memory tests is the actual number of seconds 
required to complete the task, with timing terminated and 60 seconds recorded if per- 
formance is not completed within that allotted time. The number of pictured objects 
not correctly recalled or recognized is the score recorded for the three memory subtests. 
This is the scoring proposed in the SKT manual (Erzigkeit, 1989b) and has been the 
basis for norms developed from German-language administrations. We will refer to it 
as “timed scoring” to distinguish it from scoring based on the “quality of response,” 
which was added in the clinical trials for linopirdine, the Du Pont Merck drug (DuP-996) 
in the United States. For this report, the scores that originally were recorded as time 
in seconds were divided by a scale factor of 10 to equate approximately the scoring ranges 
and variances of the memory and attention subtests. 
Quality of response refers to accuracy of performance and is intended to distinguish 
important differences in performance that are not distinguishable in the time score alone. 
In the efforts to distinguish differences in performance, a distinction is drawn between 
a simple error and a gross error. A “simple errror” is defined as a mistake made by a 
patient who is able to understand and generally is following instructions for the task 
at hand. A “gross error” is of such proportion that a patient could not complete the 
task without redirection and readministration of the instructions. The quality of response 
ratings for the non-memory tasks are defined on a 5-point scale as follows: 1 = com- 
pleted task without error; 2 = one or two simple errors; 3 = several (3-5) simple errors 
or one gross error with or without 1-2 simple errors; 4 = more than 5 simple errors, 
one gross error plus 3-5 simple errors, or two or more gross errors; 5 = cannot under- 
stand instructions and/or cannot perform independently. For the three memory subtests 
of immediate and delayed recall and recognition, quality of response is the number of 
items not correctly recalled or recognized. Because the quality of response ratings was 
recorded in real time as the test administrator also timed performance and concentrated 
on the speeded test procedure, it was accepted that the category descriptions would serve 
only as guidelines for clinical ratings, rather than definitions. One of the purposes of 
this report is to examine whether the quality scores add appreciably to the information 
obtained from timed scoring of the SKT.

YONG S. KIM AND DONALD W. NIBBELINK
Du Pont Merck Pharmaceutical Co.
University of Texas Medical School

be .75 and .93. Test-retest reliability of the composite SKT total score was
.90. The correlations between the SKT memory and attention factor scores
and the MMSE and ADAS measurements of dementia also support validity
with regard to the broader construct of cognitive dysfunction.

from German-speaking countries consistently have revealed two primary factors inter-
preted as representing independent dimensions of memory and attention.

for use of the SKT in dementia research in the United States. First, essentially all publica-
tions have been in the German language, and many are in sources not readily available
to American investigators. Second, and perhaps more important, a question remains
with respect to adaptation of the instrument for an English-speaking population. Erzigkeit
(1989b) has produced an English version of the SKT Manual that has had limited

A unique, and to some psychometricians questionable, feature of the SKT is its
timed administration and scoring. Individuals are instructed and prompted to perform
as rapidly as they can. A maximum of 60 seconds is allowed for each subtest. The score
recorded for all subtests except the three memory tests is the actual number of seconds
required to complete the task, with timing terminated and 60 seconds recorded if per-
formance is not completed within that allotted time. The number of pictured objects
not correctly recalled or recognized is the score recorded for the three memory subtests.
This is the scoring proposed in the SKT manual (Erzigkeit, 1989b) and has been the
basis for norms developed from German-language administrations. We will refer to it
as “timed scoring” to distinguish it from scoring based on the “quality of response,”
which was added in the clinical trials for linopirdine, the Du Pont Merck drug (DuP-996)
in the United States. For this report, the scores that originally were recorded as time
in seconds were divided by a scale factor of 10 to equate approximately the scoring ranges
and variances of the memory and attention subtests.
Quality of response refers to accuracy of performance and is intended to distinguish
important differences in performance that are not distinguishable in the time score alone.
In the efforts to distinguish differences in performance, a distinction is drawn between
a simple error and a gross error. A “simple errror” is defined as a mistake made by a
patient who is able to understand and generally is following instructions for the task
at hand. A “gross error” is of such proportion that a patient could not complete the
task without redirection and readministration of the instructions. The quality of response
ratings for the non-memory tasks are defined on a 5-point scale as follows: 1 = com-
pleted task without error; 2 = one or two simple errors; 3 = several (3-5) simple errors
or one gross error with or without 1-2 simple errors; 4 = more than 5 simple errors,
one gross error plus 3-5 simple errors, or two or more gross errors; 5 = cannot under-
stand instructions and/or cannot perform independently. For the three memory subtests
of immediate and delayed recall and recognition, quality of response is the number of
items not correctly recalled or recognized. Because the quality of response ratings was
recorded in real time as the test administrator also timed performance and concentrated
on the speeded test procedure, it was accepted that the category descriptions would serve
only as guidelines for clinical ratings, rather than definitions. One of the purposes of
this report is to examine whether the quality scores add appreciably to the information
obtained from timed scoring of the SKT.
The SKT was used for an objective assessment of the efficacy of a new drug (Du
Pont Merck DuP-996) that was being evaluated for treatment of senile dementia of the
Alzheimer type in a multicenter study that involved 265 patients in 18 study sites
throughout the United States. The study included a standard randomized, double-blind,
parallel-group design. Each patient who qualified by diagnostic criteria and severity of
dementia for entry into the treatment phase of the protocol was administered the SKT
three times before treatment actually began, once at a preliminary screening visit and
twice during a 2-week baseline period of the study. The two latter administrations are
used here to evaluate factor structure, reliability, and validity. Each patient met the Na-
tional Institute of Neurological and Communicative Disorders and Stroke (NINCDS)
criteria for senile dementia of the Alzheimer type, had Mini-Mental State Examination
(MMSE) within the range 14 to 23 (Folstein, Folstein, & McHugh, 1975), and did not
have concomitant disease or abnormal laboratory findings that should preclude safe and
effective treatment of dementia by a beneficial drug. Additional baseline data included
duplicate administrations of the Alzheimer’s Disease Assessment Scale (ADAS; Rosen,
Principal axes factor analysis with normalized varimax rotation was employed to
evaluate the factor structure of the nine subtests of the SKT battery. Separate analyses
were performed on the “timed scores” and the “qualitative scores.” The analyses were
repeated for the two baseline assessments separately and for scores averaged across
the two assessment periods. Each matrix of intercorrelations with unities in the princi-
pal diagonal first was subjected to principal components analysis to obtain eigenvalues
whose magnitudes and pattern were used to determine the number of factors to be
rotated. Both the eigenvalues greater than one criterion (Kaiser, 1961) and the scree
criterion of Cattell (1978) were considered in arriving at the determination that two
common factors were required to explain the patterns of intercorrelations for both types
of scores. Out of speculative interest in what other sources of variance might be present,
but not salient enough to define reliable factors, a three-factor solution also was
After the decision to rotate two factors, the principal axes method of factor analysis
was applied to the 9 x 9 matrices of intercorrelation with iterated communality estimates
in principal diagonals. The normalized varimax rotation was examined and found to
produce an orthogonal simple structure in two factors for each type of scoring.
Factor scores of the SKT were defined as the simple unweighted means of the scores
for subtests that projected primarily on each factor, with the time scores (seconds) first
divided by a scaling factor of 10 to provide approximately equal scoring ranges and
variances for the timed and qualitative scores, as well as for the memory and attention
factors that resulted from the analyses (Cattell, 1978, p. 299). Such simple factor scor-
ing is customary when the variables that are scored for a factor have reasonably com-
parable variances, and it is supported by relatively uniform factor loadings. The SKT
total scores for this investigation were calculated as the simple sum of subtest scores
for timed and qualitative scoring, with time scaled as indicated above.
Reliabilities of the nine subtests, two factor scores, and the SKT total score were
estimated by the test-retest method across the two baseline assessments for both timed
and qualitative scoring. According to the factor analysis model, the total variance of
a test is composed of a common component, a specific component, and random measure-
ment error. The common and specific components together constitute the reliable
variance. Accordingly, the average communality estimate derived from the factor analyses
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January 1993, Vol. 49, No. I
of the two baseline assessments separately was accepted as the estimate of common
variance in each of the nine subtests. The difference between test-retest reliability and
communality was calculated as a measure of the specific variance of each subtest. Co-
efficient alpha replaced communality as a measure of the shared variance among subtests
that were scored for each factor and for the SKT total score. Coefficient alpha is at-
tenuated by the presence of specific variance for subtests that are not truly parallel.
However, as the number of subtests that contribute to a factor score increases, coefficient
alpha internal consistency should approach test-retest reliability even in the presence
of some specific subtest variance. This analysis examined whether factor representation
in the SKT battery is adequate to provide such convergence.
Factor analyses were accomplished on correlation matrices from the two baseline
assessments separately and on a correlation matrix calculated from subtest scores that
first were averaged across the two baseline assessments. The factor structures for the
combined assessment periods should be a more stable representation of the primary
dimensions that underlie SKT test performance, and those will be presented for time-
scored data and for the qualitative scoring, together with their respective communality
estimates. However, to correspond to reliability estimates, which pertain to a single test
administration even though they are calculated by the test-retest method, communality
estimates from factor analyses of data from the two separate baseline assessment periods
were averaged. Those averaged, single administration values are used to partition the
reliable variance into common and specific components.
Concurrent validity of the SKT subtests, factor scores, and total score was examined
with reference to two instruments that are used widely to assess severity of dementia in
Alzheimer-type patients. The Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE; Folstein, Folstein,
& McHugh, 1975) is a 10-item mental status examination that surveys areas of memory,
orientation, language, and concentration, plus an additional constructional praxis task.
The Alzheimer’s Disease Assessment Scale (ADAS; Rosen et al., 1984) consists of 11
mental status subtests that are said to measure the four primary manifestations of cogni-
tive dysfunction in Alzheimer’s disease: memory, orientation, verbal fluency, and praxis.
The ADAS “Cognitive Score” is calculated as a simple sum of scores for the 1 1 cognitive
subtests. Product-moment correlation coefficients were calculated to evaluate the agreement
of each of the SKT measures with MMSE and ADAS assessments of dementia severity. In
recognition of the fact that error of measurement attenuates correlation coefficients, even
if tests actually measure the same thing, the observed correlations also were subjected to
disattenuation using reliability coefficients for SKT measures calculated for this study and
assuming reliabilities of .8 and .9 for the MMSE and ADAS, respectively. This aimed at
determining the extent to which SKT measures of severity would correlate with MMSE
and ADAS if it were not for random measurement error (Lord & Novick, 1968, p. 69).
To examine the similarities and differences in information value of the timed scor-
ing and qualitative scoring, product-moment correlations between the two types of scores
were calculated for the six time-scored subtests (disregarding memory subtests for which
error scoring was similar), for the factor scores calculated to represent the non-memory
factor from each type of scoring, and for the SKT total score based on the two types
of scoring. The interest here was in the similarity or difference in what is measured by
the two types of scoring, so these correlations were calculated from raw data averaged
across the two baseline assessment periods to minimize random measurement error. To
examine whether qualitative scoring contributed significantly to assessment of the severity
of dementia, over and above time scoring, the partial correlations between qualitative
scores and the MMSE and ADAS measures were calculated.
Principal axes factor analysis was performed to evaluate the factor structure of
the SKT subtests. The varimax-rotated orthogonal factor pattern matrix is presented
in Table 1 for timed scoring on the top and qualitative scoring on the bottom, The correla-
tion matrix that was analyzed in each case was calculated from raw scores that were
averaged across the two baseline assessments. Final iterated communality estimates for the
two-factor solutions are provided for each type of data. The three memory subtests, which
include immediate and delayed recall plus recognition memory, have uniformly high load-
ings on factor 1 for both scoring procedures. All of the remaining subtests, for which time
of performance is the time score that is recorded, have significant loadings of somewhat
more variable magnitude on factor 2 with the exception of the object naming subtest.
Simple structure characteristics appear adequate to support the independence of the
underlying factors, although the generally positive cross-loadings suggest a weak correla-
tion between the memory and attention dimensions, which will result in a modest positive
correlation between factor scores that are calculated as simple means of the subtest scores
related to each factor. Qualitative scores for the object naming task did not project sig-
nificantly on either factor, and it is interesting to note that the numeral naming task had
the next lowest communality in the two-factor solutions. This is the basis for speculating
that language fluency would appear as a third independent dimension of cognitive impair-
ment in the SKT if a third representation of that ability were present in the test battery.
Factor Structure of the SKT Test Battery
Note. -Underlined scores indicate to which factor an item is loaded.
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January 1993, Vol. 49, No. 1
Means and standard deviations for the nine subtests, the two factor scores, and
SKT total score are presented in raw-score form in Table 2 for the timed scoring on
the left and the qualitative scoring on the right. The two factor scores were calculated
as the means of subtests that project significantly on each factor, whereas the total score
is the sum of the nine subtest scores. The means and standard deviations will be con-
sidered for relevance in estimating sample sizes for future treatment evaluation studies
Means and Standard Deviations for SKT Subtests and Factors
Test-retest reliability coefficients, communality estimates, and specificity estimates
are presented for the nine subtests of the SKT battery for both the standard timed scor-
ing and the qualitative scoring in Table 3. Test-retest and coefficient alpha internal con-
sistency reliability coefficients are provided for the two factor scores and the SKT total
score for the two types of scoring. The reliability estimates for the subtests are more
variable in these data than has been reported previously. The fact that communality
estimates relate more highly to test-retest reliabilities for some subtests than for others
implies differential specificities of the different subtests. The qualitative scoring results
in greater specificity, whereas the timed scoring reveals greater communality on the
average. In spite of variable subtest reliabilities, the composite factor scores and SKT
total score provided moderate to highly adequate reliability values for both time and
qualitative scoring. Considering the inherent variability of phenomena such as “atten-
tion,” as well as the well-known day-to-day variability in performance of Alzheimer-
type patients, these latter values are viewed as quite acceptable for dementia research.
Correlation coefficients that relate timed scores to qualitative scores are presented
in Table 4 for the nine subtests, the two factor scores, and the total scores. The dis-
attenuated coefficients that estimate the correlation that should exist if it were not for
measurement error are presented in the right-hand column.
Correlation coefficients that relate timed scores to the MMSE and ADAS measures
of severity of dementia are presented in the upper section of Table 5 for the two factor
Reliability Estimates, Communalities, and Specific Variations for SKT Subtests and Factor Scores
Correlations between the “Timed” and “Qualitative” Scorings of SKT Subtests and Factors
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January 1993, Vol. 49, No. I
scores and the total score. Correlation coefficients that relate factor and total scores
calculated from qualitative scoring to the MMSE and ADAS measures of severity of
dementia are presented in the lower section of Table 5 .  In each case, disattenuated
correlation coefficients are presented in the right hand column to provide an estimate
of what the respective correlations would be if it were not for random measurement error.
Correlations of SKT to the MMSE and ADAS Measures
The SKT is a brief dementia test battery that was developed in Germany, and
essentially all of the previous psychometric documentation is derived from its use in
German-speaking Europe. Although the test consists of nine subtests that involve per-
formance tasks similar to those in more extended neuropsychological test batteries, its
brevity and time-based scoring are novel among tests previously available in the United
States. If it is to be accepted as a credible research tool in this country, there is need
for English-language documentation of its psychometric characteristics. The interpreta-
tion of performance speed, as measuring attention/concentration, needs to be validated
further against other measures in carefully diagnosed Alzheimer patients. To this end,
factor structure, common and specific subtest content, reliability, and concurrent validity
have been examined herein using data from two spaced administrations in a sample of
265 patients who met NINCDS criteria for senile dementia of the Alzheimer type.
To evaluate the validity of objective stopwatch scoring as a means of assessing levels
of cognitive dysfunction in patients with dementia, a quality of response rating was used
to characterize the accuracy of performance on each of the tasks for which time to com-
pletion was the standard SKT scoring. In addition, concurrent validity of both types
of scoring (timed and qualitative) was evaluated by comparison with scores from two
of the most widely used instruments for assessment of the severity of dementia in con-
temporary clinical research in the United States.
Both the Kaiser-Guttman’s eigenvalue criterion and the Cattell’s scree criterion con-
firmed the presence of two primary factors that underlie SKT subtest scores. This was
true for qualitative scoring as well as for the timed scoring. Two factors satisfied both
criteria in separate analyses of subtest correlations from the two baseline assessment
periods, as well as for the factor analysis of subtest scores averaged across the two ad-
ministrations for each patient. The uniformity of configurations between administra-
tions based on the two-factor solution for qualitative scoring, as well as for timed scar-
ing, appears to support the interpretation that the time measurements reflect something
more cognitively relevant than speed of performance alone.
The similarities of factor structures based on qualitative and timed scoring suggest
that the response-time measurements represent a primary cognitive impairment in pa-
tients with dementia. It has been the tradition in neuropsychological testing in the United
States, and particularly in clinical mental status evaluation of cognitive dysfunction in
Alzheimer’s disease, to focus on the quality or accuracy of performance. The fact that
the timed scores and the qualitative scores related in similar ways to the two underlying
factors is further suggestion that a common underlying mechanism may be involved.
The similarities of the factor structures for the U.S. data and those for German
data reported in the SKT Manual constitute impressive evidence of the transcultural
stability of the instrument. No less than six factor analyses of independent German/
Austrian samples uniformly have produced two factors, and the subtest projections con-
sistently have segregated the three “memory” subtests and the six subtests that relate
to a second factor that has been interpreted as “attention.” The factor analysis by Overall
and Schaltenbrand (1992) provided conformation of the same factor structure in an
English-language publication, although the data that were analyzed originated in
Germany. The fact that a highly similar factor structure for the timed scoring was ob-
tained in the analysis of the data in the U.S. suggests that the SKT is applicable well
in both languages. The fact that similar patterns of factor loadings derived from timed
scoring and qualitative scoring suggests that similar underlying cognitive dimensions
are being measured in different ways.
Although only two primary factors have over-determined representation in the SKT
test profiles, the differential communalities and specificities suggest that the naming tasks
(pictured objects and numerals) are perhaps least influenced by attentionlconcentra-
tion difficulties. This appears particularly true in qualitative rating of performance on
the object-naming task, although the residual specificities in Table 3 appear remarkably
similar for the two naming tasks. Also, the difference between factor loadings for timed
scoring and qualitative scoring on the object naming subtests suggests that some
qualitative difference is represented in object-naming performance. We are familiar with
the Boston Naming Test (Goodglass & Kaplan, 1983), as well as with the fact that verbal
fluency is widely conceived as a distinct, neurologically based impairment. If word finding
and related aspects of verbal fluency were represented by additional subtests, it is quite
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January 1993, Vol. 49, No. I
likely that they would define a separate performance dimension. The specific variances,
not accounted for by communalities, confirm that these subtests measure distinct im-
pairments in addition to the common factor of attention. If the specific variations were
redundantly represented in the test battery, no doubt other common factors could be
defined. In that regard, the “attention” mechanism can be conceived as a more pervasive
higher-order dimension that is involved to some extent in a multiplicity of primary per-
formances. Attention also is recognized to be important for primary memory, but in
the SKT, as in other dementia batteries, the specific ability of memory is given enough
redundant representation to define a separate factor. Also, memory is a focal dysfunc-
tion in the Alzheimer population that has an early appearance in the temporal course
of the disorder, which would tend to segregate its associated variance in a cross-sectional
sample that includes patients in varying stages of the disease.
The test-retest reliabilities of the nine individual subtests are moderate in most cases.
That is particularly true of the qualitative ratings. However, the correlations among
subtest scores are sufficient to provide evidence of substantial dependence on two com-
mon factors, and the combining of subtest scores into composite factor scores and a
total score enhances reliability. Nevertheless, this raises a question about the meaning
of “reliability” for measurements that are as exact as stopwatch timing permits. Reliability
of clinical ratings often is conceived as “random measurement error” in judgment and
the recording of observations. The objectivity and accuracy of stopwatch measurement
are intended to eliminate that aspect of unreliability . The “reliability” of these and other
measurements pertains to the accuracy in quantifying the true level of the cognitive or
behavioral substrate of interest. In that regard, the distinction between concepts of
reliability and validity tend to blur, as do state and trait concepts. Exact stopwatch
measurements differ between closely spaced independent assessments, and test-retest
reliability is an index of the stable average differences between individuals relative to
the trial-to-trial variability within individuals. In case of timed scores, test-retest reliability
refers to the stability of the underlying cognitive process as evident in objective time
measurements taken at an approximate 14-day interval.
The test-retest reliability of qualitative performance ratings is affected by the same
variations in underlying cognitive processes as are the timed ratings, but in addition
there is an element of judgmental error and recording error in the ratings. It is little
wonder that they appear slightly less reliable. However, a question remains with regard
to validity, or the accuracy with which each type of measurement reflects the underly-
ing cognitive process of interest. It is obvious to an observer that time of performance
is affected by lapses of attention, but time is also partially a function of “cognitive
processing speed” and motor components in the presence of full attention. Accuracy
of performance is obviously also a function of attention, but it seems even more suscep-
tible to other cognitive processing capabilities.
Correlations between timed scoring and qualitative scoring, as well as correlations
between those two types of scoring and external measures of severity of dementia
represented in the MMSE and ADAS, are important in separating the reliability and
validity issues. The correlations between timed scoring and qualitative scoring were
modestly lower than the respective reliability estimates for those two types of measure-
ments. However, when the correlations were disattenuated for unreliability of measure-
ment, they tended to approach unity for most subtests and particularly for the two factor
scores. This is, we believe, the strongest evidence that the timed scoring and qualitative
scoring measure largely the same underlying cognitive abilities in the Alzheimer popula-
tion except for measurement error. The correlations between the SKT memory and
attention factor scores and the MMSE and ADAS measurements of severity of dementia
also appeared sufficient to support validity in relation to the broader construct of cognitive
dysfunction. The total scores calculated from SKT timed scoring and qualitative scoring
appeared highly related to the general dementia process as measured by the other two
instruments. When these correlations were disattenuated for unreliability, the evidence
suggests that both standard SKT timed scoring and qualitative scoring are valid measures
of the generalized cognitive dysfunction associated with Alzheimer’s disease.
